caveats: a: The occupation table is for four-digit NAICS 311900 and includes branded and larger manufacturers outside the lens.
a: Occupation shares are employment-weighted rather than wage-weighted; the range is a judgmental task decomposition.
a: Exposure is not equivalent to full-job elimination and excludes already-automated work.
rho: Current AI-use surveys are cross-sector or all-manufacturing and define use more broadly than realized labor substitution.
rho: The NIST adoption figure relies on cited external survey work and includes chatbots, not only factory-floor implementation.
rho: FDA traceability requirements apply only to covered foods and can create compliance work as well as automation opportunity.
e: No source measures the contract/private-label share of LMM firms in NAICS 311999.
e: Public-company channel evidence may not represent private LMM firms.
e: Eligibility may change materially with the threshold used for recurring outsourced revenue.
s5: This is a bounded hazard estimate, not an observed industry transfer rate.
s5: Small private transactions are underreported and NAICS coding in deal databases is noisy.
s5: Regulatory problems, customer concentration, or deferred capex can make an apparent firm non-transferable.
q: Pricing terms vary by customer, ingredient volatility, formulation ownership, and capacity scarcity.
q: The cited public-company disclosures are examples, not a measured distribution for NAICS 311999 LMM contracts.
q: The estimate excludes demand volume loss and implementation failure, which belong in other primitives.
d5: The USDA series covers all food at home and away from home, far broader than miscellaneous contract manufacturing.
d5: Constant-dollar expenditure is an imperfect proxy for constant-quality physical quantity.
d5: The public-company contract channel is adjacent and one-year growth is volatile.
o: This is an operator-necessity judgment, not a measured substitution rate.
o: The FDA traceability rule covers specified foods, not every 311999 product.
o: Alternative physical manufacturers count as operator-required supply even if the incumbent loses the customer.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: B2B production programs and repeat purchase orders for food made to customer specifications; revenue is generally per unit or batch, with pricing that may embed conversion fees, ingredient and packaging costs, or negotiated cost pass-throughs.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to contract and private-label manufacturers because NAICS 311999 combines own-account branded product makers with firms performing recurring outsourced production; only the latter form a coherent population under the fixed service lens.
lens.excluded_activities: Own-brand manufacturers without a recurring outsourced-production relationship, captive plants, pure distributors and brokers, farms, software vendors, non-control financing vehicles, and food activities classified outside NAICS 311999.
lens.included_activities: Independent US contract and private-label manufacturers in NAICS 311999 that repeatedly formulate, blend, process, package, or label miscellaneous shelf-stable food for external brand, retail, foodservice, or ingredient customers, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: BLS measures the employment and wage mix for broader NAICS 311900, not wage-weighted task exposure in 311999 contract manufacturers. I map high exposure to clerical, planning, forecasting, documentation, customer-service, and analytical tasks; partial exposure to quality inspection, scheduling, and maintenance diagnostics; and low exposure to physical line operation and material handling. Broader-industry inclusion of more automated and larger plants could overstate digital readiness, while occupation-level treatment understates exposed tasks embedded in production roles.
caveats: The occupation table is for four-digit NAICS 311900 and includes branded and larger manufacturers outside the lens.
Occupation shares are employment-weighted rather than wage-weighted; the range is a judgmental task decomposition.
Exposure is not equivalent to full-job elimination and excludes already-automated work.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll-by-occupation and time-and-motion data from at least 15 NAICS 311999 contract plants, then code tasks against deployed document AI, machine vision, planning, and predictive-maintenance capabilities; reconcile to actual labor hours and wages.
a.rationale: In broader other-food manufacturing, production occupations are 43.62% of employment, office and administrative support 7.15%, installation/maintenance 6.04%, management 5.69%, business and financial operations 3.64%, and sales 2.64%. AI can materially assist documentation, planning, forecasting, customer communications, visual quality checks, and predictive maintenance, but most mixing, handling, sanitation, changeover, and line work remains physical. Wage weighting raises the influence of the smaller managerial and professional groups, yielding a base exposed share near one-fifth.

d5.bridge: USDA measures constant-dollar spending on the entire US food basket, and a public nut processor reports one year's contract-manufacturing volume, not constant-quality quantity for NAICS 311999 outsourced production. I use the broad food series to anchor resilient end demand and the contract-channel disclosure only as evidence that outsourced volume can grow, then narrow the five-year range around flat-to-modest real quantity growth. Spending may overstate quantity if quality mix rises, while one-company channel growth overstates durable industry growth.
caveats: The USDA series covers all food at home and away from home, far broader than miscellaneous contract manufacturing.
Constant-dollar expenditure is an imperfect proxy for constant-quality physical quantity.
The public-company contract channel is adjacent and one-year growth is volatile.
d5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of shipment volume by 311999 product and channel from Circana, NielsenIQ, customer procurement data, and contract manufacturers; separate price, package-size, quality mix, wins/losses, and outsourcing share, then obtain customer five-year sourcing plans.
d5.rationale: Inflation-adjusted total US food spending rose from $2.49 trillion in 2024 to $2.51 trillion in 2025 and has grown over the long term. In one adjacent public manufacturer, contract-manufacturing dollar sales rose 8.4% and volume 23.3% in fiscal 2025, though partly from a new customer and acquisition effects. Food demand is defensive, but miscellaneous categories face health trends, customer churn, formulation shifts, and insourcing. The base therefore assumes about 2% more constant-quality outsourced quantity in year five, with a downside contraction and modest upside.

caveats: No source measures the contract/private-label share of LMM firms in NAICS 311999.
Public-company channel evidence may not represent private LMM firms.
Eligibility may change materially with the threshold used for recurring outsourced revenue.
e.change_evidence: Build a firm-level census of the 120 dataset-estimated LMM firms using websites, customer disclosures, certification directories, and interviews; classify each firm's revenue share from third-party contract/private-label programs and require a majority or strategically material recurring outsourced stream.
e.rationale: The official category spans disparate products and does not distinguish own-brand plants from outsourced manufacturers. Contract and private-label channels clearly exist, including repeated customer programs and co-manufacturing, but many firms primarily manufacture their own branded or commodity products and therefore fail the external outsourced-service lens. The range treats roughly one-fifth to just under one-half of LMM firms as qualifying, with 30% as the working midpoint.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311999 ($3,111,261,386) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311999 ($18,042,485,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $564,163 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311999); class <5: 326 firms, avg revenue/firm $705,123 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,531 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 122 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,957,967 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $298,590 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 107 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,967,252 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $757,506 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,352,850 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,408,810 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $80,913,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,339,266 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $237,871,214 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $36,275,360 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 120

caveats: This is an operator-necessity judgment, not a measured substitution rate.
The FDA traceability rule covers specified foods, not every 311999 product.
Alternative physical manufacturers count as operator-required supply even if the incumbent loses the customer.
o.change_evidence: Survey current customers on insourcing plans, capital budgets, minimum efficient scale, qualification time, and willingness to replace contract plants with automated internal lines; review contract termination and plant-insourcing history by product category.
o.rationale: The output is physical food produced under customer specifications, food-safety controls, traceability obligations, sanitation regimes, and accountable release decisions. Software can automate records, planning, inspection, and support, but cannot eliminate the licensed/certified operating entity, plant assets, supply chain, physical transformation, and product-liability accountability. Some customers can insource or switch to self-manufacture, which keeps the estimate below one.

caveats: Pricing terms vary by customer, ingredient volatility, formulation ownership, and capacity scarcity.
The cited public-company disclosures are examples, not a measured distribution for NAICS 311999 LMM contracts.
The estimate excludes demand volume loss and implementation failure, which belong in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: Review customer MSAs, purchase orders, bid calendars, cost-change clauses, open-book requirements, volume rebates, most-favored pricing, and renewal histories across at least 20 customer relationships; interview procurement managers on expected sharing of conversion-cost savings.
q.rationale: Implemented savings can be retained initially through fewer hires, lower scrap, better scheduling, and reduced downtime, especially inside a fixed unit price. Retention erodes as sophisticated brand and retail customers rebid programs, demand open-book costing, negotiate productivity, or exert competitive pricing pressure. Public filings show both recurring contract-manufacturing growth and meaningful competitive pricing pressure, so a discounted five-year retained share near one-third is more defensible than assuming either full pass-through or permanent capture.

rho.bridge: Census reports any-function AI use across US businesses and NIST reports adoption and barriers across manufacturers, rather than the five-year implemented share of exposed labor in small NAICS 311999 contract plants. I translate current broad adoption, rising expected use, and documented legacy-system, skills, cost, data, and cybersecurity barriers into a midpoint where about half of exposed opportunity is operationalized. Broad definitions that include chatbots likely overstate deep workflow implementation, while the five-year horizon and focused post-acquisition investment push the other way.
caveats: Current AI-use surveys are cross-sector or all-manufacturing and define use more broadly than realized labor substitution.
The NIST adoption figure relies on cited external survey work and includes chatbots, not only factory-floor implementation.
FDA traceability requirements apply only to covered foods and can create compliance work as well as automation opportunity.
rho.change_evidence: Review five-year digital roadmaps, MES/ERP readiness, machine-vision pilots, validated savings, exception rates, and retraining plans at a representative sample of LMM contract food plants; conduct QA-leader interviews on acceptable human-review thresholds.
rho.rationale: Census found 18%-20% of firms using AI in late 2025 through May 2026 and 20%-23% expecting use within six months, with larger firms adopting more. NIST identifies manufacturing applications in quality, forecasting, scheduling, and maintenance but also data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-integration barriers. Food traceability duties require linked records and rapid production of data, increasing the payoff to digital workflows while making validation and governance important. A disciplined operator can implement meaningful assistive automation within five years, but hygienic controls, fragmented systems, customer specifications, and QA accountability prevent full conversion.

caveats: This is a bounded hazard estimate, not an observed industry transfer rate.
Small private transactions are underreported and NAICS coding in deal databases is noisy.
Regulatory problems, customer concentration, or deferred capex can make an apparent firm non-transferable.
s5.change_evidence: Match five years of GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and Capital IQ transaction records to a verified roster of eligible NAICS 311999 contract manufacturers; supplement with owner-age and succession-plan interviews from lenders, brokers, and plant owners.
s5.rationale: No industry-specific denominator links eligible LMM contract food manufacturers to qualifying control transfers. The range corresponds to a sustained annual qualifying-transfer hazard of roughly 2.5%-6.4%, allowing for founder succession, strategic purchases, and sponsor activity while excluding minority financings, internal reorganizations, closures, and non-transferable operations.

principal_driver: Physical, repeat outsourced production remains necessary while document-heavy planning, traceability, service, quality, and maintenance workflows offer targeted AI leverage.
principal_weakness: The eligible population is a minority hidden inside a heterogeneous NAICS code, and powerful customers can reclaim efficiency gains through repricing or rebids.

sources.supports: Official scope and illustrative products for the heterogeneous residual food-manufacturing category.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 311999 All Other Miscellaneous Food Manufacturing

sources.supports: Broader other-food-manufacturing occupation mix, including 259,650 total jobs, 43.62% production, 7.15% office/administrative, 6.04% installation/maintenance, 5.69% management, 3.64% business/financial, and 2.64% sales.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 311900

sources.supports: BTOS evidence that overall business AI use was 17%-20% from December 2025 to May 2026, expected six-month use was 20%-23%, and adoption rose with firm size.
sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users

sources.supports: Manufacturing AI applications in quality control, predictive maintenance, demand forecasting, resource management, and scheduling; reported adoption and barriers including data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system integration.
sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing: Text Only

sources.supports: Covered manufacturers must maintain key data for critical tracking events, maintain a traceability plan, and provide relevant records or an electronic sortable spreadsheet to FDA within 24 hours; enforcement is deferred until July 20, 2028.
sources.title: FSMA Final Rule on Requirements for Additional Traceability Records for Certain Foods

sources.supports: USDA constant-dollar food expenditure evidence: total spending rose from $2.49 trillion in 2024 to $2.51 trillion in 2025, with long-run growth in both food at home and away from home.
sources.title: Total U.S. food spending reached $2.51 trillion in 2025

sources.supports: Adjacent public-company evidence that contract-manufacturing net sales rose 8.4% and volume 23.3% in fiscal 2025, while commodity costs and competitive pricing pressure affected margins.
sources.title: John B. Sanfilippo & Son, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Example of outsourced-food manufacturing economics with a manufacturing fee tied to total product costs, quarterly cost review, reimbursement mechanics, and limits on annual conversion and overhead cost increases.
sources.title: Manufacturing and Supply Agreement filed with the SEC

title: All Other Miscellaneous Food Manufacturing
